retrieved_docs.title: Middle Ear Cholesteatoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Canal wall-up procedures generally offer better cosmetic outcomes, avoid the need for mastoid bowl cleaning, and can result in better hearing. However, they may have a higher risk of persistent or recurrent cholesteatoma. Canal wall-down procedures have their own advantages and disadvantages, including but not limited to: Need for more frequent ear cleaning. Inability to withstand water exposure with possible resultant vertigo. Potentially less favorable hearing results, particularly if ossicular chain reconstruction is required. Difficulty fitting hearing aids postoperatively. Potentially lower rate of persistent or recurrent cholesteatoma or need for a second-look procedure. The decision to proceed with a canal wall-down approach is often made intraoperatively at the surgeon's discretion. Factors that may prompt conversion to a canal wall-down procedure include:

retrieved_docs.title: Mastoidectomy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who are medically unfit for elective surgery should not undergo mastoidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Tympanoplasty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tympanoplasty is the surgical procedure performed to repair a perforated TM, with or without reconstruction of the ossicles (ossiculoplasty), aiming to prevent reinfection and restore hearing ability. [2] CSOM is the most common indication; large invasive cholesteatomas may require a mastoidectomy as well as reconstruction of the TM. [5] [8] The history of tympanoplasty began in the 1950s when Wullstein and Zollner popularized the technique of using overlay graft to reconstruct the perforated TM and restore the sound conduction apparatus of the middle ear. [9] Since then, surgical approaches to tympanoplasty have been modified, as described herein.

retrieved_docs.title: Round And Oval Window Reinforcement -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Autophony is defined as hearing one’s voice or other bodily functions such as pulse, eye movements, and limb movements as loud or distorted. [18] Autophony is most often related to third window pathologies such as various semicircular canal dehiscence syndromes, perilymphatic fistula, or an enlarged vestibular aqueduct. The proposed mechanism of autophony is that energy of the offending bodily sounds is mechanically transmitted through a pathological third window directly into the cochlea, thus overriding sounds being transmitted by the outer and middle ear. It has been demonstrated that reinforcing the natural windows, usually only the round window, returns the labyrinthine system to 2 windows and may improve autophony. However, this approach has not been extensively studied, and results are varied. [19] Systematic reviews have suggested that primary window reinforcement is best employed as a first-line, low-risk intervention in medically complex patients not suitable for more invasive management of third window pathologies, such as transmastoid or middle cranial fossa craniotomy approaches to superior semicircular canal dehiscence.

retrieved_docs.title: Mastoidectomy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mastoidectomy is a surgical procedure of the temporal bone that opens postauricular air cells by removing the thin bony partitions between them. Each mastoidectomy is unique because of the variable pneumatization patterns of the temporal bone. Pathology can also limit the pneumatization of the mastoid cells and further complicate the mastoidectomy procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Mastoidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The wound is typically then closed with absorbable deep sutures to bring the periosteum back together. An additional layer of buried interrupted sutures is placed in the deep dermal layer. Finally, the epidermis can be approximated either by a superficial running layer or by a tissue adhesive. A Glasscock dressing can then be placed over the ear for one to two days.

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the anatomy of the tympanic membrane. Identify the indications for a tympanoplasty. Summarize the most commonly used surgical approaches for tympanoplasty. Describe common postoperative complications of tympanoplasty. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ossiculoplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Postauricular Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Ossiculoplasty in chronic ear surgery typically takes place as the final step following a tympanomastoidectomy and the removal of cholesteatoma or other pathology. The middle ear should be well exposed at this point, allowing for ossiculoplasty and tympanoplasty.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Mastoidectomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: There are multiple indications for mastoidectomy. The common philosophy in mastoid surgery is to create a safe and dry ear. The main indications for performing a mastoidectomy include acute mastoiditis, chronic mastoiditis with its sequelae, and cholesteatoma. [1] Mastoidectomy can be performed in coordination with tympanostomy tube placement in patients with complications of chronic otitis media or acute otitis media. Mastoidectomy also serves as a key surgical approach for many otological procedures, including labyrinthectomy, endolymphatic sac surgery, facial nerve surgery, cochlear implantation, petrous apex lesions, and lateral skull base tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Ossiculoplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Postauricular Approach
retrieved_docs.content: If a tympanomastoidectomy is not performed and a posterior auricular approach is selected, incisions similar to those in the transcanal approach are made to enable the elevation of a tympanomeatal flap. A posterior auricular incision is created that extends down to a plane just lateral to the temporalis fascia. An additional incision through the musculoperiosteum is made along the temporal line, along with a perpendicular musculoperiosteal incision behind the cartilaginous ear canal and directed toward the mastoid tip.

retrieved_docs.title: Tympanoplasty -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Prior to surgery, a number of investigations assessing middle ear functionality are undertaken – the results can, in turn, influence both the tympanoplasty technique used and also the postoperative outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Ossiculoplasty -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Establishing a stable, well-aerated middle ear environment is critical for ossiculoplasty success. A contracted middle ear space can restrict the movement of the tympanic membrane, ossicular chain, and round window, resulting in a 35- to 55-dB conductive hearing loss. [33] Middle ear pathology resulting from poor ventilation is significantly more detrimental to ossiculoplasty success than surgical technique and prosthetic factors. [34] [35] Additionally, conditions such as negative middle ear pressure, inadequate mastoid pneumatization, fluid accumulation, ongoing Eustachian tube dysfunction, granulation tissue, middle ear fibrosis, adhesive disease, and tympanic membrane perforations can all impact the effectiveness of sound transmission.

retrieved_docs.title: Mastoidectomy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the anatomy involved in a mastoidectomy. Identify the indications for a mastoidectomy. Explain the technique of a mastoidectomy. Describe interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to enhance the care of patients that undergo mastoidectomy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Mastoidectomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who undergo mastoidectomy for acute mastoiditis may avoid complications of mastoiditis and will benefit from a faster clearance of the infection compared to antibiotics alone. Patients who undergo mastoidectomy for chronic diseases, such as infection or cholesteatoma, experience relief from chronic ear drainage and irritation. They, more often than not, will experience some improvement in hearing. They also avoid complications of chronic disease such as extemporal spread, especially toward the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2775
retrieved_docs.content: The term ekonomika is recognized as an allowed qualifier for a wide spectrum of medical procedures and contexts, indicating that economic considerations are applicable across diverse aspects of patient care. Specifically, ekonomika may be applied to both temporary and permanent colostomy and to the conversion of cardiac rhythm, reflecting its relevance to major surgical and cardiac interventions. In addition, ekonomika is an allowed qualifier for diagnostic and monitoring techniques such as myelogram and telemetrie, as well as for common otolaryngologic surgeries including tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy and tympanoplasty. The qualifier also extends to non‑procedural events, being applicable to a حادث مروري (traffic accident) and to dental extraction (قلع الأسنان). Together, these relationships underscore that economic factors are systematically considered in the planning, execution, and evaluation of a broad range of clinical procedures, diagnostic tests, and injury‑related care.

retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier links each of the following medical procedures and conditions—colostomy (temporary and permanent), conversion of cardiac rhythm, diagnostic amniocentesis, nephrotomy, milk substitutes, chest tube insertion (استِرواح الصَّدْر), spinal cord fracture (كسر في النخاع الشوكي), organ transplantation (other than bone marrow), personality disorders, tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy, tympanoplasty, and hemipelvectomy (بتر حوضي شقي)—to the category “ekonomika.” Recognizing these interventions as allowed qualifiers under “ekonomika” provides a unified framework for categorizing diverse clinical services within health‑care economic and administrative contexts.

retrieved_docs.content: The “allowed qualifier” is a permissible descriptor within a medical classification framework that can be attached to a wide range of diagnostic, therapeutic, surgical and event‑coding entities to convey contextual information such as historical data (“dějiny”), economic considerations (“ekonomika”) or formal coding classification (“klasifikace”). Historical qualifiers are permitted for tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy, tympanoplasty, illegal induced abortion (إجهاض محرض غير قانوني), application of a medical loop (تطبيق علقة طبية), coronary artery imaging (تَصويرُ الأَوعِيَة التاجية), traffic accident (حادث مروري), tooth extraction (قلع الأسنان), arthrocentesis, cerebral arteriogram, temporary and permanent colostomy, conversion of cardiac rhythm, diagnostic amniocentesis, diagnostic spinal tap and e‑codes indicating place of occurrence. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 822
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s medical knowledge is organized around two principal classification nodes—**dějiny** and **ekonomika**—which serve as cross‑language, cross‑coding hubs linking a wide spectrum of diagnostic, therapeutic and surgical interventions, conditions, and coding concepts. Both nodes share qualifiers such as **arthrocentesis**, **cerebral arteriogram**, **temporary and permanent colostomy**, **conversion of cardiac rhythm**, **diagnostic amniocentesis**, **nephrotomy**, **nephrostomy**, and **spinal fusion** (the latter appearing in both nodes). Dějiny additionally connects procedures including **excision of the semilunar cartilage of the knee**, **temporary and permanent gastrostomy**, **immunoglobulin therapy**, **myelogram**, **organ transplantation (excluding bone‑marrow)**, **other respiratory therapy**, **rheumatoid arthritis care**, **tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy**, **tympanoplasty**, **bone‑marrow transplantation**, **traffic accident care**, **bronchial and tracheal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1541
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier “ekonomika” is applied to a diverse set of medical interventions and conditions, indicating that these procedures are recognized within an economic framework for budgeting, reimbursement, or resource allocation. Specifically, “ekonomika” pertains to nephrotomy, spinal cord fracture, myelogram, organ transplantation (other than bone marrow), personality disorders, tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy, tympanoplasty, and cerebrospinal fluid shunt surgeries. This inclusion underscores that each of these services is considered permissible or eligible for economic evaluation in healthcare planning and patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is used as a linking designation for a broad range of medical interventions and conditions in the context of ekonomika. In this framework, the procedures conversion of cardiac rhythm and diagnostic amniocentesis are specifically connected to ekonomika. Conversely, ekonomika is associated with several additional surgical and diagnostic actions, including nefrotomy, كسر في النخاع الشوكي (spinal fracture), myelogram, organ transplantation (other than bone marrow), tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy, tympanoplasty, and عمليات تحويل السائل الدماغي النخاعي (cerebrospinal fluid diversion surgeries). This set of relationships indicates that the concept of ekonomika serves as a central term under which these diverse medical procedures are considered permissible or relevant according to the Allowed qualifier classification, encompassing cardiac rhythm management, prenatal diagnostic testing, renal surgery, spinal injury assessment, neuroimaging, transplantation, ENT surgery, middle‑ear reconstruction, and CSF diversion procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is linked to conversion of cardiac rhythm, a procedure commonly referred to as cardioversion, and is also associated through the term dějiny with a diverse set of medical concepts and interventions. These include gastro­stomy (both temporary and permanent), myelogram, psychological and psychiatric evaluation and therapy, radionuklidy (radioisotopes used in diagnostic and therapeutic contexts), tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy, and tympanoplasty. In addition, Allowed qualifier connects to several Arabic‑language entities: اتصال جنسي مغاير (heterosexual intercourse), اسْتِرواحُ الصَّدْر (thoracentesis or pleural aspiration), تطبيق علقة طبية (application of a medical loop), and حادث (accident). Together, these relationships illustrate that the generic label “Allowed qualifier” can be applied to a wide range of cardiac, surgical, diagnostic, therapeutic, psychological, and situational medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1299
retrieved_docs.content: The “Allowed qualifier” is presented as a unifying classification that groups a wide array of medical procedures, therapies, diagnostic studies, monitoring techniques, and external events for purposes such as coding, billing, eligibility, and cost assessment. Within this framework, both surgical and non‑surgical interventions are included: colostomy (temporary and permanent), conversion of cardiac rhythm, nephrotomy, organ transplantation (excluding bone marrow), other respiratory therapy, radionuklidy (radioisotope therapy), tonsillectomy and/or adenoidectomy, tympanoplasty, tooth extraction (قلع الأسنان), thoracic aspiration (اسْتِرْواحُ الصَّدْر), cerebral concussion (الارتجاج المخي), cerebrospinal fluid shunt surgeries (عمليات تحويل السائل الدماغي النخاعي), and traffic accident (حادث مروري). Diagnostic and interventional imaging procedures such as myelogram and fluoroscopic examination of the biliary and pancreatic ducts (ERCP) are also covered, as is the patient‑monitoring modality telemetry (telemetrie). The qualifier is linked to the economic dimension “ekonomika,” indicating that each listed intervention is considered with respect to cost or reimbursement factors. Simultaneously, it is associated with the systematic categorization “klasifikace,” which provides a structured taxonomy for the same set of procedures; for example, colostomy, conversion of cardiac rhythm, ERCP, and nephrotomy are each tied to this classification system. Overall, the “Allowed qualifier” integrates clinical scope, economic considerations, and formal classification, offering a comprehensive framework for understanding the permissibility, financial implications, and systematic organization of diverse medical interventions across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1472
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a concept that can be applied to a variety of medical and clinical contexts. It serves as a permissible descriptor for procedures such as conversion of cardiac rhythm and diagnostic amniocentesis, indicating that these interventions may be performed under specific qualifying conditions. In addition, the term dějiny, which denotes “history,” is linked to a broad spectrum of events and procedures relevant to patient care and public health. Dějiny is associated with heterosexual sexual contact (اتصال جنسي مغاير), both temporary and permanent gastrostomy, myelogram examinations, organ transplantation procedures that exclude bone marrow, and tympanoplasty for middle‑ear reconstruction. It also relates to sensitive and legally significant situations such as illegal induced abortion (إجهاض محرض غير قانوني) and traffic accidents (حادث مروري). Together, these connections illustrate how allowed qualifiers and the historical context of dějiny intersect with diverse medical interventions, diagnostic techniques, surgical procedures, and socio‑legal circumstances, underscoring the importance of clear qualification criteria in clinical decision‑making and documentation.

The klasifikace qualifier permits a subset of the same interventions—cerebral arteriogram; temporary and permanent colostomy; conversion of cardiac rhythm; diagnostic amniocentesis; temporary and permanent gastrostomy; nephrotomy; chest drainage (اسْتِرْواحُ الصَّدْر); and cerebral concussion—providing a structured coding
